220	
221	1. Read your directive completely.

1	\chapter{Abelian-variety rigidity: morphisms from a genus-$0$ curve into an abelian variety are constant}
2	\label{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean
4	
5	% Local macro: diagrammatic (Lean `≫`) composition. Defined locally because this
6	% chapter is the writer's only editable file; flagged in the writer report for promotion
7	% to macros/common.tex if desired. \providecommand is a no-op if it is later added globally.
8	\providecommand{\fatsemi}{\mathbin{\,;\,}}
9	
10	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-157). This chapter is the dedicated home of the project's COMMITTED
11	% genus-0 route (route (c)): the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity stack. It is
12	% 1:1 with the new upstream Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, which
13	% imports AlgebraicJacobian.Genus and is imported by AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian (breaking the
14	% RigidityKbar -> Rigidity -> Jacobian import cycle so that genusZeroWitness can consume the
15	% keystone directly). The headline thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV is the char-free
16	% replacement (no [CharZero]) for the over-k-bar declaration rigidity_over_kbar of
17	% \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and
18	% still carries [CharZero]). The df=0 / H^0(P^1,Omega)=0 / Serre-duality framing is the
19	% fallback (a), NOT the route developed here.
20	
21	The genus-$0$ arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
22	($J = \Spec k$); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
23	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$
24	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
25	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field $k$, so the
26	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
27	O(-2)) = 0$, hence $df = 0$, hence $f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
28	characteristic-$p$ Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant $f$ can have $df = 0$. The
29	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
30	
31	The minimal chain has four links. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
32	Mumford, Form~I) is an elementary properness/closed-map argument --- it uses neither the
33	theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is the entry point for formalisation. The
34	\emph{theorem of the cube} (\cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}) enters only as the deep,
35	characteristic-free input that an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb
36	P^1$ (the single-curve base case of Milne's Proposition~3.10); it is recorded here as a
37	\textbf{deferred} prerequisite with no formal proof yet. Combining the two gives that every
38	morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing

40	$C \cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$) yields the headline
41	\cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. This chain invokes neither Serre duality nor the FGA

66	  Let $X$ be a complete variety, and let $Y$, $Z$ be any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
67	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism such that for some $y_0 \in Y$ the image

69	  $g \colon Y \to Z$ with $f = g \circ p_2$, where $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
70	  projection; that is, $f$ depends only on the $Y$-coordinate, and $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ for any
71	  fixed $x_0 \in X$.

115	  Fix any point $x_0 \in X$ and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ (the
116	  composite of $y \mapsto (x_0, y)$ with $f$). Since $X \times Y$ is a variety (in
117	  particular reduced and irreducible, so separated and with no embedded structure), to prove
118	  the equality of morphisms $f = g \circ p_2$ it suffices to prove they agree on a non-empty
119	  open subset of $X \times Y$.
120	
121	  Let $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, put $F = Z - U$ (a closed set),
122	  and set $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$. Because $X$ is complete, the projection
123	  $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of
124	  completeness/properness); hence $G$, the image of the closed set $f^{-1}(F)$, is closed in
125	  $Y$. The hypothesis $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U$ gives
126	  $f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing$, so $y_0 \notin G$. Therefore
127	  $V := Y - G$ is a non-empty open subset of $Y$.
128	
129	  For each $y \in V$ we have $X \times \{y\} \cap f^{-1}(F) = \varnothing$, i.e.
130	  $f(X \times \{y\}) \subseteq U$. Thus the complete (proper) variety $X \times \{y\}$ is
131	  mapped by $f$ into the \emph{affine} variety $U$. A proper connected variety mapping to an
132	  affine variety has image a single point (a global regular function on a complete connected
133	  variety is constant). Hence $f(X \times \{y\})$ is one point, necessarily
134	  $f(x_0, y) = g(y)$. So for all $x \in X$ and $y \in V$,
135	  \[ f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g(y) = (g \circ p_2)(x, y). \]
136	  The two morphisms $f$ and $g \circ p_2$ thus agree on the non-empty open
137	  $X \times V$, which is dense in the irreducible $X \times Y$; as $Z$ is separated they
138	  agree everywhere. \qedhere

204	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}

206	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
207	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let $X$ be a complete variety, $Y$ and $Z$
208	  % any varieties, and $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ a morphism such that for some
209	  % $y_0 \in Y$, $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0$ of $Z$. Then there is a

212	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
213	  dense-open construction is the body of that proof).}
214	  Let $X$ be a complete variety and $Y$, $Z$ any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
215	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism, fix a point $x_0 \in X$, and suppose given
216	  $y_0 \in Y$, $z_0 \in Z$ with the \emph{collapse hypothesis}
217	  \[ f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \qquad
218	     \text{(in Lean: } \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathbf 1_X)\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi y_0) \fatsemi f
219	     = \mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi z_0\text{).} \]
220	  Then there is a non-empty open subset $U \subseteq X \times Y$ on which $f$ agrees, as a
221	  scheme morphism, with the collapsed map $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$, where
222	  $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,
223	  (p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y)$ is the ``$(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$'' endomorphism. Concretely
224	  $U = X \times V$ with $V := Y - G$, $G = p_2(f^{-1}(Z - U_0))$ for an affine open
225	  neighbourhood $U_0$ of $z_0$, and on $U$ one has $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y)$.
226	
227	  \medskip
228	  \noindent\textbf{The collapse hypothesis is load-bearing.} The non-emptiness of $V$ is exactly
229	  the assertion $y_0 \notin G$, and this holds \emph{because} $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}
230	  \subseteq U_0$ keeps the slice over $y_0$ inside the affine $U_0$, away from $F = Z - U_0$. A
231	  version of this lemma \emph{without} the collapse hypothesis is false: for $X = Y = Z$ and
232	  $f = p_1$ the first projection, $f$ and $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f \colon (x, y) \mapsto x_0$
233	  agree on no non-empty open (the agreement locus is contained in $\{x = x_0\}$, which has empty
234	  interior in the irreducible $X \times Y$). Therefore the formal target \emph{must} carry the
235	  collapse hypothesis as an explicit antecedent; dropping it (as an earlier decomposition
236	  silently did) makes the deferred goal unsatisfiable.
237	
238	  \medskip
239	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization note: algebraically closed base, and locally-of-finite-type
240	  source.} The prose already takes $\bar k$ algebraically closed, but the formal statement must
241	  \emph{encode} two properties. First, the cohomology-free proof of the slice-constancy bridge

245	  per-slice agreement (Step~2 of the bridge, \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}) needs the

247	  Over the algebraically closed $\bar k$ this holds precisely because $X \times Y$ is \emph{locally of

249	  property of $\Spec \bar k$, and \texttt{closure\_closedPoints} then gives density); this requires

668	  unirational variety is $\mathbb P^1$ itself. We isolate the genuine content.
669	
670	  \textbf{Reduction to a base point.} Translating in the group $A$, we may assume $f(\infty) =
671	  0_A$ for a chosen point $\infty \in \mathbb P^1$. The map $f$ is a morphism out of the
672	  complete nonsingular variety $\mathbb P^1$, so there is no indeterminacy to resolve (a
673	  rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety is everywhere defined, Milne
674	  Theorem~3.2; here $f$ is already a morphism).
675	
676	  \textbf{The base case is where the depth lies.} The constancy of $f$ is the statement that
677	  an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb P^1$ --- ``$A$ contains no
678	  rational curves''. Over an arbitrary field this does \emph{not} follow from
679	  \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} alone: the Rigidity Lemma collapses a \emph{product}
680	  $X \times Y \to Z$ when one coordinate axis is already collapsed, but for a single map
681	  $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ no coordinate axis is collapsed a priori (the difference map
682	  $(x, y) \mapsto f(x) - f(y)$ on $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1$ is constant on an axis only
683	  if $f$ is already constant). The characteristic-free input that supplies the missing fact
684	  is the theorem of the cube (\cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}): it forces the additive
685	  structure of $\Pic$ and, with it, the triviality of the line bundles a nonconstant

687	  characteristic $0$ one may instead pull back an invariant differential and use
688	  $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = 0$; that argument fails in characteristic $p$, which is exactly
689	  why the cube is the committed characteristic-free engine.)

691	  \textbf{Conclusion.} Granting the base case, $f$ is constant with value $f(\infty) = 0_A$
692	  (before the normalising translation, the constant value is the original $f(\infty)$). For

744	  sub-build --- Mathlib has no Riemann--Roch for curves).}
745	
746	  Over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$ every closed point is $\bar k$-rational, so $C$
747	  has at least two distinct $\bar k$-points $P \neq Q$ (a smooth proper geometrically
748	  irreducible curve over $\bar k$ is infinite). With $\genus(C) = \dim_{\bar k}
749	  H^1(C, \mathcal O_C) = 0$, apply the Riemann--Roch theorem to the degree-$0$ divisor
750	  $D = P - Q$. Since $\deg(K - D) = 2g - 2 - \deg D = -2 < 0$ one has $l(K - D) = 0$, so
751	  Riemann--Roch $l(D) - l(K - D) = \deg D + 1 - g$ gives $l(D) = 0 + 1 - 0 = 1$. Thus the
752	  degree-$0$ divisor $D$ is linearly equivalent to an effective divisor, forcing $D \sim 0$,

The minimal chain has four links. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
Mumford, Form~I) is an elementary properness/closed-map argument --- it uses neither the
theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is the entry point for formalisation. The
\emph{theorem of the cube} (\cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}) enters only as the deep,
characteristic-free input that an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb
P^1$ (the single-curve base case of Milne's Proposition~3.10); it is recorded here as a
\textbf{deferred} prerequisite with no formal proof yet. Combining the two gives that every
morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing
with the classification of genus-$0$ curves (\cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1},
$C \cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$) yields the headline
\cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. This chain invokes neither Serre duality nor the FGA
$\Pic$ representability engine, so the genus-$0$ arm is fully decoupled from Route~A.

The chain has two parts. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, Mumford,
Form~I; equivalently Milne's Rigidity Theorem~1.1) is an elementary properness/closed-map
argument --- it uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, is the entry point
for formalisation, and is now proven axiom-clean. From the Rigidity Lemma, Milne's \S I.3
derives the genus-$0$ base case \emph{without} the theorem of the cube, through a short
additive-structure chain: additivity of $\mathrm{Hom}(-, A)$ over a product
(\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5); the fact that a pointed regular
map of abelian varieties is a homomorphism (\cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}, Milne
Corollary~1.2); the everywhere-definedness of a rational map from a nonsingular variety into
an abelian variety (\cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}, Milne Theorem~3.2); and the
$\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility that forces a pointed $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ to be
constant (\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}, the core of Milne Proposition~3.9). Combining these
gives that every morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant
(\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing with the classification of genus-$0$
curves (\cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1}, $C \cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$) yields the
headline \cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. The theorem of the cube (Milne \S I.5) is
\emph{not} used anywhere on this path (see \cref{rmk:cube_not_needed}); the chain invokes
neither Serre duality nor the FGA $\Pic$ representability engine, so the genus-$0$ arm is
fully decoupled from Route~A.

\section{The Milne \S I.3 chain: additivity, homomorphisms, extension}

This section assembles, from the proven Rigidity Lemma, the three Milne \S I.3 corollaries
that supply the genus-$0$ base case. None of them uses the theorem of the cube; each is a
direct consequence of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} (or, for the extension lemma, of the valuative
criterion of properness). The output is the $\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility lemma
\cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}, which is exactly the content of Milne's Proposition~3.9 ``every
rational map $\mathbb P^1 \dashrightarrow A$ is constant''.

\begin{remark}
  \label{rmk:cube_not_needed}
  \textbf{The theorem of the cube is not needed on the genus-$0$ path.} An earlier draft of
  this chapter recorded the theorem of the cube as a deferred prerequisite of the single-curve
  base case ``$\mathbb P^1 \to A$ constant''. That was a misreading. Milne's \S I.3 derives the
  base case (Proposition~3.9, the $d = 1$ case of Proposition~3.10) directly from the Rigidity
  Theorem~1.1 --- our \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} --- via Corollary~1.5 (additivity), Corollary~1.2
  (regular maps of abelian varieties are homomorphisms up to translation), and Theorem~3.2
  (rational maps from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extend), then a
  $\mathbb G_a$ vs.\ $\mathbb G_m$ group-law incompatibility. The theorem of the cube (Milne
  \S I.5) enters only for projectivity, the seesaw/square principle, dual abelian varieties,
  polarizations, and the Poincar\'e sheaf --- none on the genus-$0$ path. For confirmation: the
  Albanese universal property that the positive-genus Route~A needs (Milne Proposition~6.1/6.4,
  \S III.6) is \emph{also} cube-free, resting on the same Theorem~3.2 + Corollary~1.2/1.5
  ingredients.
\end{remark}

% V and W. On points, f(v) = h(v,w0) and g(w) = h(v0,w), and so φ =df h − (f∘p + g∘q) is the
% map that sends (v,w) ↦ h(v,w) − h(v,w0) − h(v0,w). Thus φ(V×{w0}) = 0 = φ({v0}×W) and so the
% theorem shows that φ = 0." [Milne, Cor 1.5, \S I.1, doc p.~9--10.]
\begin{proof}
  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma}
  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.5.}
  Define $f := h|_{V \times \{w_0\}}$ and $g := h|_{\{v_0\} \times W}$, identifying
  $V \times \{w_0\} \cong V$ and $\{v_0\} \times W \cong W$; on points $f(v) = h(v, w_0)$ and
  $g(w) = h(v_0, w)$. Form the difference
  \[ \varphi := h - (f \circ p + g \circ q) \colon V \times W \to A, \qquad
     \varphi(v, w) = h(v, w) - h(v, w_0) - h(v_0, w) \]
  (the subtraction is in the group $A$, i.e.\ $\varphi$ is the composite of
  $(h, -(f \circ p + g \circ q))$ with the group law of $A$). Then $\varphi$ vanishes on both
  axes: $\varphi(V \times \{w_0\}) = 0 = \varphi(\{v_0\} \times W)$ (direct computation, using
  $h(v_0, w_0) = 0$). In particular $\varphi$ collapses the complete axis $V \times \{w_0\}$ to
  the single point $0 \in A$, so by the Rigidity Lemma (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}, with $V$
  complete and $V \times W$ geometrically irreducible) $\varphi$ factors through the projection
  $q$; but it also vanishes on $\{v_0\} \times W$, which is a section of $q$, so the factoring
  morphism is identically $0$, i.e.\ $\varphi \equiv 0$. Hence $h = f \circ p + g \circ q$.
  Uniqueness: restricting any such decomposition to the axes recovers $f$ and $g$ as the
  axis-restrictions of $h$.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[A pointed regular map of abelian varieties is a homomorphism (Milne Corollary 1.2)]
  \label{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.av_regularMap_isHom_of_zero}
  \uses{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}
  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Corollary 1.2, \S I.1, doc p.~9 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 15)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "COROLLARY 1.2. Every regular map α:A→B of abelian varieties is the composite
  % of a homomorphism with a translation."
  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.2.}
  Every regular map $\alpha \colon A \to B$ of abelian varieties over $\bar k$ is the composite
  of a homomorphism with a translation; equivalently, if $\alpha(0_A) = 0_B$ then $\alpha$ is a
  homomorphism, i.e.\ $\alpha(a + a') = \alpha(a) + \alpha(a')$ for all $a, a' \in A$.
\end{lemma}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. The regular map α will send the k-rational point 0 of A to a
% k-rational point b of B. After composing α with translation by −b, we may assume that
% α(0)=0. Consider the map φ:A×A→B, φ(a,a′)=α(a+a′) − α(a) − α(a′). By this we mean that φ is
% the difference of the two regular maps A×A →(m)→ A →(α)→ B and A×A →(α×α)→ B×B →(m)→ B, which
% is a regular map. Then φ(A×0)=0=φ(0×A) and so φ=0. This means that α is a homomorphism."
% [Milne, Cor 1.2, \S I.1, doc p.~9.]
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}
  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Corollary~1.2.}
  The image $\alpha(0_A)$ is a $\bar k$-point $b$ of $B$; composing $\alpha$ with translation by
  $-b$ we may assume $\alpha(0_A) = 0_B$, and it suffices to show such an $\alpha$ is a
  homomorphism. Form
  \[ \varphi \colon A \times A \to B, \qquad \varphi(a, a') = \alpha(a + a') - \alpha(a) - \alpha(a'), \]
  the difference of the two regular maps $A \times A \xrightarrow{m} A \xrightarrow{\alpha} B$
  and $A \times A \xrightarrow{\alpha \times \alpha} B \times B \xrightarrow{m} B$. Then
  $\varphi(0_A, 0_A) = 0$ and $\varphi$ vanishes on both axes,
  $\varphi(A \times 0) = 0 = \varphi(0 \times A)$ (since $\alpha(0) = 0$). By the additive
  decomposition \cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product} (with $V = W = A$), the axis-restrictions
  of $\varphi$ being $0$ force $\varphi \equiv 0$; equivalently, $\varphi$ collapses the complete
  axis $A \times 0$ and vanishes on $0 \times A$, so Rigidity gives $\varphi = 0$. Hence
  $\alpha(a + a') = \alpha(a) + \alpha(a')$, i.e.\ $\alpha$ is a homomorphism.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
[A rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety extends (Milne Theorem 3.2)]
  \label{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rationalMap_to_av_extends}
  \uses{}
  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Theorem 3.2 (with Theorem 3.1 and Lemma 3.3), \S I.3, doc p.~16--18 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF pages 22--24)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "THEOREM 3.2. A rational map α:V ⇢ A from a nonsingular variety to an abelian
  % variety is defined on the whole of V."
  % SOURCE QUOTE (Thm 3.1, used in the proof): "THEOREM 3.1. A rational map φ:V ⇢ W from a
  % normal variety V to a complete variety W is defined on an open subset U of V whose
  % complement V∖U has codimension ≥ 2."
  % SOURCE QUOTE (Lemma 3.3, used in the proof): "LEMMA 3.3. Let φ:V ⇢ G be a rational map from
  % a nonsingular variety to a group variety. Then either φ is defined on all of V or the points
  % where it is not defined form a closed subset of pure codimension 1 in V (i.e., a finite
  % union of prime divisors)."
  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Theorem~3.2 (with Theorem~3.1, Lemma~3.3).}
  A rational map $\alpha \colon V \dashrightarrow A$ from a nonsingular variety $V$ over
  $\bar k$ to an abelian variety $A$ is defined on the whole of $V$.
\end{lemma}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Combine Theorem 3.1 with the next lemma." [Milne, Thm 3.2, \S I.3,
% doc p.~17.]
% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF (Thm 3.1, general case): "Let U be the largest subset on which φ is
% defined, and suppose that V∖U has codimension 1. Then there is a prime divisor Z in V∖U.
% Because V is normal, its associated local ring is a discrete valuation ring O_Z with field of
% fractions k(V). The map φ defines a morphism of schemes Spec(k(V)) → W, which the valuative
% criterion of properness (Hartshorne 1977, II 4.7) shows extends to a morphism Spec(O_Z) → W.
% This implies that φ has a representative defined on an open subset that meets Z in a nonempty
% set, which is a contradiction." [Milne, Thm 3.1, \S I.3, doc p.~17.]
% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF (Lemma 3.3, the difference-map): "Define a rational map Φ:V×V ⇢ G,
% (x,y) ↦ φ(x)·φ(y)⁻¹. ... Thus φ is defined at x if and only if Φ is defined at (x,x). ...
% Suppose φ is not defined at x. Then for some f ∈ Im(Φ*), (x,x) ∈ div(f)₁, and clearly Φ is
% not defined at the points (y,y) ∈ Δ ∩ div(f)₁. This is a subset of pure codimension one in Δ,
% and when we identify it with a subset of V, it is a subset of V of codimension one passing
% through x on which φ is not defined." [Milne, Lemma 3.3, \S I.3, doc p.~17--18.]
\begin{proof}
  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Theorem~3.2.}
  The statement is Theorem~3.1 combined with Lemma~3.3. By Theorem~3.1 (a consequence of the
  valuative criterion of properness, Mathlib \texttt{IsProper}/\texttt{ValuativeCriterion},
  applied at the discrete valuation ring of each codimension-$1$ point), a rational map from the
  normal $V$ to the complete $A$ is defined outside a closed set of codimension $\geq 2$. By
  Lemma~3.3, the indeterminacy locus of a rational map into a \emph{group} variety is either
  empty or pure of codimension $1$: forming the difference map
  $\Phi(x, y) = \varphi(x)\,\varphi(y)^{-1} \colon V \times V \dashrightarrow A$, one shows
  $\varphi$ is defined at $x$ iff $\Phi$ is defined at the diagonal point $(x, x)$, and an
  indeterminacy point produces a pure-codimension-$1$ component of the non-domain. The two
  facts are compatible only if the indeterminacy locus is empty; hence $\alpha$ is everywhere
  defined.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}
  \label{rmk:thm32_codim1_mathlib_gap}
  \textbf{Formalisation risk: the pure-codimension-$1$ step (Lemma 3.3) has no Mathlib
  Weil-divisor support.} Theorem~3.1's everywhere-extension-outside-codimension-$2$ is within
  reach of Mathlib's valuative criterion of properness
  (\texttt{ValuativeCriterion}/\texttt{IsProper.of\_valuativeCriterion}), applied at the
  discrete valuation ring $\mathcal O_{V, Z}$ of each codimension-$1$ point $Z$. But Lemma~3.3,
  which rules out codimension-$1$ indeterminacy for maps into a group variety, is proved in
  Milne via Weil-divisor theory ($\mathrm{div}(f)_0 - \mathrm{div}(f)_1$, prime divisors,
  tangent cones) for which Mathlib currently has \emph{no} usable API at this generality. This
  is the route's riskiest sub-build. An open question for the prover: whether a \emph{pointwise}
  valuative extension --- showing, at each codimension-$1$ point $Z$ separately, that the
  morphism $\Spec k(V) \to A$ extends over $\Spec \mathcal O_{V, Z}$ using only that $A$ is
  proper and a group (so the difference-map argument can be run \emph{locally} at $Z$ without
  globally building $\mathrm{div}(f)$) --- side-steps building divisor theory entirely. For the
  single application here ($V = \mathbb P^1$, already a morphism) the lemma is \emph{not even
  invoked} (see \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); it is needed only for the higher Albanese
  inputs of Route~A, so its sub-build can be deferred without blocking the genus-$0$ arm.
\end{remark}

\begin{lemma}
[The $\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$ incompatibility: a pointed $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant (core of Milne Prop 3.9)]
  \label{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}
  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.morphism_Ga_to_av_const}
  \uses{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom, lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}
  % SOURCE: [Milne, Abelian Varieties], Proposition 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20 (read from references/abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF pages 25--26)
  % SOURCE QUOTE: "PROPOSITION 3.9. Every rational map A¹ ⇢ A or P¹ ⇢ A is constant."
  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
  Let $A$ be an abelian variety over $\bar k$ and $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ a
  morphism with $f(\infty) = 0_A$. Restricted to $\mathbb G_a = \mathbb A^1 = \mathbb P^1
  \setminus \{\infty\}$, the map $f$ is an additive-group homomorphism
  $f(x + y) = f(x) + f(y)$; restricted instead to $\mathbb G_m = \mathbb A^1 \setminus \{0\}$ it
  satisfies $f(xy) = f(x) + f(y) + c$ for a constant $c$. These two structures are incompatible
  unless $f$ is constant; hence $f$ is constant.
\end{lemma}

% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. According to (3.2), α extends to a regular map on the whole of A¹.
% After composing α with a translation, we may suppose that α(0)=0. Then α is a homomorphism,
% α(x+y)=α(x)+α(y); all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. But A¹−{0} is also a group variety, and similarly,
% α(xy)=α(x)+α(y)+c; all x,y ∈ A¹(k^al)=k^al. This is absurd, unless α is constant." [Milne,
% Prop 3.9, \S I.3, doc p.~19--20.]
\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom, lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}
  \textit{Source: Milne, Abelian Varieties, Proposition~3.9.}
  Restrict $f$ to the affine line $\mathbb A^1 = \mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}$, regarded as
  the additive group variety $\mathbb G_a$ (it is everywhere defined there, and would extend
  even from a rational map by \cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}). After the normalising
  translation $f(0) = 0_A$ is arranged, $f \colon \mathbb G_a \to A$ is a regular map of group
  varieties carrying $0$ to $0$, hence a homomorphism by \cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}:
  \[ f(x + y) = f(x) + f(y), \qquad x, y \in \mathbb A^1(\bar k) = \bar k. \]
  But $\mathbb A^1 \setminus \{0\} = \mathbb G_m$ is \emph{also} a group variety, and the same
  homomorphism argument applied to the multiplicative structure gives
  \[ f(xy) = f(x) + f(y) + c, \qquad x, y \in \mathbb G_m(\bar k) = \bar k^\times, \]
  for a constant $c \in A$. The two relations are incompatible (an additive-group homomorphism
  $\mathbb G_a \to A$ that is simultaneously, up to translation, a multiplicative-group
  homomorphism $\mathbb G_m \to A$ on the overlap forces the image to be a single point), unless
  $f$ is constant. Hence $f$ is constant, with value $f(\infty)$ (the original base value before
  any translation). \qedhere
\end{proof}

\section{Morphisms from $\mathbb P^1$ to an abelian variety are constant}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{thm:rigidity_lemma, thm:theorem_of_the_cube}
  \leanok
  This is the single-factor ($d = 1$) instance of Milne's Proposition~3.10, where the
  unirational variety is $\mathbb P^1$ itself. We isolate the genuine content.

  \textbf{Reduction to a base point.} Translating in the group $A$, we may assume $f(\infty) =
  0_A$ for a chosen point $\infty \in \mathbb P^1$. The map $f$ is a morphism out of the
  complete nonsingular variety $\mathbb P^1$, so there is no indeterminacy to resolve (a
  rational map from a nonsingular variety to an abelian variety is everywhere defined, Milne
  Theorem~3.2; here $f$ is already a morphism).

  \textbf{The base case is where the depth lies.} The constancy of $f$ is the statement that
  an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb P^1$ --- ``$A$ contains no
  rational curves''. Over an arbitrary field this does \emph{not} follow from
  \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} alone: the Rigidity Lemma collapses a \emph{product}
  $X \times Y \to Z$ when one coordinate axis is already collapsed, but for a single map
  $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ no coordinate axis is collapsed a priori (the difference map
  $(x, y) \mapsto f(x) - f(y)$ on $\mathbb P^1 \times \mathbb P^1$ is constant on an axis only
  if $f$ is already constant). The characteristic-free input that supplies the missing fact
  is the theorem of the cube (\cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}): it forces the additive
  structure of $\Pic$ and, with it, the triviality of the line bundles a nonconstant
  $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ would produce, so that no such nonconstant morphism exists. (In
  characteristic $0$ one may instead pull back an invariant differential and use
  $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = 0$; that argument fails in characteristic $p$, which is exactly
  why the cube is the committed characteristic-free engine.)

  \textbf{Conclusion.} Granting the base case, $f$ is constant with value $f(\infty) = 0_A$
  (before the normalising translation, the constant value is the original $f(\infty)$). For
  the multi-factor statement of Proposition~3.10 (a general unirational $V$), one writes
  $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum_i \beta_i(x_i)$ via the additive decomposition (Milne
  Corollary~1.5, itself a corollary of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}) and applies the base case to
  each $\beta_i \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$; we need only the $d = 1$ case here.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}
  \label{rmk:cube_is_load_bearing}
  The Rigidity Lemma (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma}) is genuinely used in the surrounding
  machinery --- it yields Milne's Corollary~1.5 (additivity of $\mathrm{Hom}(- , A)$ over a
  product of complete pointed varieties), which drives the multi-factor induction of
  Proposition~3.10 and which proves that maps of abelian varieties are homomorphisms up to
  translation. But the \emph{single-curve} base case ``$\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant'' is
  not a formal consequence of the Rigidity Lemma by itself; in characteristic-free form it
  rests on \cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}. This refines the iter-156 expectation that a single
  $\mathbb P^1$ factor would be cube-free: the multi-factor \emph{induction} is cube-free
  (it is pure Rigidity-Lemma additivity), but the base of that induction is not. See the
  Strategy-modifying findings of this chapter's writer report.
\end{remark}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial}
  \leanok
  This is the single-factor ($d = 1$) instance of Milne's Proposition~3.10, where the
  unirational variety is $\mathbb P^1$ itself. It rests entirely on the Milne \S I.3 chain
  assembled above; the theorem of the cube is \emph{not} used (\cref{rmk:cube_not_needed}).

  \textbf{Reduction to a base point.} Choose a point $\infty \in \mathbb P^1$. Translating in
  the group $A$ by $-f(\infty)$, we may assume $f(\infty) = 0_A$; un-translating at the end
  restores the original value. The map $f$ is already a morphism out of the complete
  nonsingular $\mathbb P^1$, so no indeterminacy needs resolving (and would extend even from a
  rational map, by \cref{lem:rational_map_to_av_extends}).

  \textbf{The $\mathbb G_a/\mathbb G_m$ argument.} Apply \cref{lem:hom_from_Ga_trivial} to the
  pointed morphism $f$ with $f(\infty) = 0_A$: restricting to $\mathbb G_a = \mathbb A^1 =
  \mathbb P^1 \setminus \{\infty\}$ makes $f$ an additive-group homomorphism (it is a regular
  map of group varieties carrying $0$ to $0$, hence a homomorphism by
  \cref{lem:av_regular_map_is_hom}); restricting to $\mathbb G_m = \mathbb A^1 \setminus \{0\}$
  makes it satisfy $f(xy) = f(x) + f(y) + c$. The two group-law structures are incompatible
  unless $f$ is constant. Hence $f$ is constant.

  \textbf{Conclusion.} $f$ is constant with value $f(\infty) = 0_A$ in the normalised picture,
  i.e.\ the original $f(\infty)$ before translation. For the multi-factor statement of
  Proposition~3.10 (a general unirational $V$), one writes
  $\beta(x_1, \ldots, x_d) = \sum_i \beta_i(x_i)$ via the additive decomposition
  (\cref{lem:hom_additivity_over_product}, Milne Corollary~1.5) and applies this base case to
  each $\beta_i \colon \mathbb P^1 \to A$; we need only the $d = 1$ case here.
\end{proof}
